1. Introduction
1.1 Purpose of the Reading Report
This reading report expands the content of Lecture 1 from the course Managing AI Development and Infrastructure into a structured study document. The or

structure into a structured study document. The original material introduced artificial intelligence, explained why AI has become central to modern organizations, and connected the topic to infrastructure, data, computation, business adoption, education, healthcare and smart city applications. The report is written as a learning resource rather than a word-for-word transcript. It keeps the main ideas from the lecture, but the text has been organized, corrected, expanded and rewritten into cleare

zed, corrected, expanded and rewritten into clearer academic notes.
The lecture began with a welcome to a short seven-week course and a brief introduction of the instructor. The instructor explained that the course is focused on AI leadership and management, but it still includes technical exposure. This is important because leaders and managers who make decisions about AI systems do not need to become full-time programmers, but they do need enough technical understanding to communicate effectiv

gh technical understanding to communicate effectively with engineers, vendors, analysts and senior decision makers. AI projects often fail not because the algorithm is impossible, but because expectations, data requirements, infrastructure needs, costs, risks and operational processes are not understood properly at the management stage.
The overall aim of this report is therefore to convert lecture content into reading material that can support revision, weekly logs and project planning. It expl

evision, weekly logs and project planning. It explains the difference between artificial intelligence, machine learning and deep learning; discusses human intelligence as a reference point for machine intelligence; summarizes the history and future of AI; describes why data and computation are essential to AI; and provides examples of practical AI applications in different sectors. It also includes a practical exercise with a small dataset, a suggested GitHub README, key takeaways, self-assessme

gested GitHub README, key takeaways, self-assessment questions and references.
1.2 How the Reading Material Was Produced
The reading material follows the requested workflow. First, the lecture was available as a recorded video. A recorded video contains both visual information, such as slides, and audio information, such as the instructor narration and student questions. For text-based study notes, the most important input is usually the audio track because it contains the spoken explanation. Th

ack because it contains the spoken explanation. The video can therefore be converted into an audio file, commonly in MP3 or WAV format. Once the audio is available, an automatic speech recognition system can convert the audio into raw text.
In this case, the recording was processed into six transcript parts. The raw transcript contained the instructor explanation, student questions and conversational wording. Like most automatic transcripts, it needed editing because speech recognition systems c

eeded editing because speech recognition systems can mishear technical words, miss punctuation, combine sentences incorrectly or produce informal wording. The transcript was therefore cleaned and expanded into coherent sections. This stage is not simply grammar correction. It also requires understanding the lecture topic, identifying the main concepts, grouping related ideas under headings and adding explanatory links between ideas.
The final stage was to extend the notes into a complete reading

ge was to extend the notes into a complete reading report of more than 5000 words. This expansion adds definitions, examples, tables, a diagram-style workflow, a practical activity and revision material. The report does not aim to replace the lecture recording. Instead, it is designed to make the lecture easier to read, revise and apply. A student can use this document to understand the main content before writing a weekly log, selecting a project topic or discussing AI applications in a workpla

t topic or discussing AI applications in a workplace context.
Table 1. Workflow used to convert the lecture recording into structured reading notes.
2. Course Overview and Learning Context
2.1 Course Structure
The course described in the lecture is a seven-week course. Each week focuses on a different AI topic and gradually moves from general introduction to more applied management and infrastructure issues. Week 1 introduces AI and its applications. Week 2 focuses on data analysis and feature e

ons. Week 2 focuses on data analysis and feature engineering for AI applications. Week 3 covers data preprocessing, data processing and AI algorithms. Week 4 addresses AI ethical frameworks, generative AI and agentic AI for business integration. Week 5 focuses on responsible AI and AI infrastructure setup. Week 6 discusses scaling infrastructure for AI applications and price estimation. Week 7 covers AI infrastructure security setup and monitoring.
This structure is useful because AI development

g.
This structure is useful because AI development is not only about choosing an algorithm. A successful AI project requires an understanding of the problem, the data, the model, the infrastructure, the risk and the operational environment. For example, a company may want to introduce an AI chatbot for customer support. The basic idea may sound simple, but the real project involves many questions. What data will train or guide the chatbot? Where will the model be hosted? How will private informa

will the model be hosted? How will private information be protected? Who will monitor incorrect answers? What will the system cost at scale? How will the organization measure whether the chatbot improves performance? These questions show why AI leadership needs both management thinking and technical awareness.
The instructor also emphasized that students may come from diverse professional backgrounds. Some may have technical experience in computing, while others may work in supply chain, busines

ng, while others may work in supply chain, business, healthcare, education, operations or management. The course therefore tries to balance technical and non-technical explanations. Coding is not a required assessment component, but optional examples and demonstrations may be shared so that students who want deeper exposure can explore how AI systems work behind the scenes.
2.2 Assessment Structure
The course uses weekly logs and team projects. Seven weekly logs are individual tasks, with each l

even weekly logs are individual tasks, with each log contributing a small percentage to the final grade. The logs are designed to help students reflect on weekly content and connect lecture concepts to examples. The first log asks students to briefly explain the difference between artificial intelligence and machine learning, especially focusing on how data is used, and to explain how the difference is experienced through business applications with two examples.
The course also includes group pr

th two examples.
The course also includes group projects. Teams are expected to have three members, although exceptional arrangements may be discussed with the instructor. The same team continues from Project 1 to Project 2 because the two projects are connected. Students can choose from suggested topics or propose a topic related to their own industry, provided the topic meets the basic requirements of an AI application. This is valuable because AI is most meaningful when it is connected to rea

AI is most meaningful when it is connected to real problems. A student working in supply chain might study demand forecasting or autonomous forklifts. A student in education might study personalized learning systems. A student in healthcare might examine diagnostic support or administrative automation.
The assessment design encourages collaboration, communication and practical application. AI development in organizations is rarely an individual activity. It normally involves managers, subject m

activity. It normally involves managers, subject matter experts, data engineers, developers, cybersecurity staff, legal teams and end users. By working in teams, students practice the kind of communication and planning required in real AI projects.
Table 2. Summary of the seven-week course structure discussed in Lecture 1.
3. From Human Intelligence to Artificial Intelligence
3.1 Human Learning as a Model for AI
A central idea in the lecture was the comparison between human intelligence and arti

the comparison between human intelligence and artificial intelligence. The instructor used the example of how a baby learns. A newborn does not begin life with language, decision-making ability or detailed memory. Over time, the child repeatedly sees people, hears words, observes objects and receives feedback. Through continuous exposure, the child begins to recognize parents, respond emotionally, speak words, identify objects and eventually make decisions. This is a useful analogy for AI becaus

decisions. This is a useful analogy for AI because many AI systems also depend on repeated exposure to data.
In human learning, experience plays a major role. A child learns the word for an object because the object and the word are repeatedly presented together. The brain builds memory and associations. Similarly, machine learning systems identify patterns from training data. If an image recognition system is shown many labeled pictures of cats and dogs, it gradually learns visual differences

and dogs, it gradually learns visual differences between them. If a speech recognition system is trained on thousands of hours of audio and text pairs, it learns to map sound patterns to words. If a language model is trained on large amounts of text, it learns statistical patterns of language and can generate responses that appear fluent.
The analogy is not perfect. Human intelligence includes consciousness, emotion, physical experience, social understanding and moral reasoning. AI systems do n

understanding and moral reasoning. AI systems do not possess these human qualities in the same way. However, the analogy helps explain why training data is so important. Without experience, a child cannot develop knowledge. Without data, an AI model cannot learn useful patterns. This is why the lecture described data as fuel for AI applications.
3.2 AI Compared with Human Intelligence
Artificial intelligence is created by humans, whereas human intelligence develops naturally through biological p

telligence develops naturally through biological processes. AI systems can process certain types of data much faster than humans. A calculator or computer can multiply large numbers almost instantly, while a human may need time. AI can also analyze millions of records or images much faster than a human team. This speed is one reason organizations use AI for search engines, fraud detection, recommendation systems and large-scale monitoring.
However, AI systems are usually highly objective or task

r, AI systems are usually highly objective or task-focused. A model trained to detect brain tumors from MRI scans cannot automatically drive a car or manage a warehouse. It may perform very well on one specific task but fail outside that task. Humans, by contrast, can move across many activities in daily life. A person can attend a class, think about an assignment, drive home, cook food, hold a conversation and adapt to unexpected events. AI has become more general with large language models, bu

become more general with large language models, but most AI systems still depend strongly on the purpose, data and context for which they were designed.
The lecture also compared accuracy, adaptability, energy use, multitasking, social skills and innovation. AI can be highly accurate in narrow applications, but it may struggle when conditions change or when the input is outside its training distribution. Humans may be slower or less precise in some calculations, but they are flexible, socially a

me calculations, but they are flexible, socially aware and able to adapt to new situations. AI systems can optimize existing processes, but human beings continue to define goals, interpret values and create new directions for innovation.
Table 3. Comparison of artificial intelligence and human intelligence.
4. Core AI Concepts
4.1 Problem Solving, Reasoning, Planning and Learning
The lecture defined intelligence as the ability to solve problems. In the context of AI, problem solving means that a

In the context of AI, problem solving means that a system can identify a suitable solution for a given task. Sometimes there may be many possible solutions, and the system must choose the best or most efficient one. For example, a route planning system may compare many possible paths and select the fastest route based on distance, traffic and road conditions. A warehouse optimization system may decide how to move goods efficiently. A medical decision support tool may rank possible diagnoses base

sion support tool may rank possible diagnoses based on symptoms and test results.
Reasoning means explaining or justifying a decision. In modern AI, this connects with the idea of explainable AI. Many AI models can produce outputs, but users also need to understand why the output was produced. For example, if an AI system rejects a loan application, the applicant and the bank may need to know which factors influenced the decision. If a medical AI tool flags an image as high risk, the doctor may

tool flags an image as high risk, the doctor may need to know which region of the image contributed to that result. Reasoning is therefore important for trust, safety, accountability and regulation.
Planning means identifying the steps needed to solve a problem. A planning system does not only provide a final answer; it also determines a sequence of actions. In a business context, this might involve planning inventory levels for the next month. In robotics, it may involve deciding how a robot s

In robotics, it may involve deciding how a robot should move from one position to another without hitting obstacles. In project management, AI could help break down a project into tasks, dependencies and timelines.
Learning means improving performance through data or experience. AI systems are rarely perfect after the first version. Models are tested, monitored, updated and improved. The lecture used the example of ChatGPT and image generation. Early versions of AI image systems often produced u

arly versions of AI image systems often produced unreadable text inside images, but later systems improved significantly. This demonstrates that AI development is not a one-time event. It is an ongoing process of training, evaluation, feedback and improvement.
Table 4. Key elements of intelligence discussed in the lecture.
4.2 Symbolic AI, Machine Learning and Deep Learning
The lecture separated AI into several connected areas. Symbolic AI is one of the earliest forms. It uses explicit rules, su

of the earliest forms. It uses explicit rules, such as if-then statements, to make decisions. For example, a simple rule-based weather system might state: if temperature is above 30 degrees and humidity is above 70 percent, then there may be a chance of rain. Symbolic AI can be useful when rules are clear and stable, but it becomes difficult when there are too many rules or when the environment changes constantly.
Machine learning is different because the system learns patterns from data instea

ecause the system learns patterns from data instead of relying only on rules written by a programmer. In a standard machine learning project, data scientists often identify features from the data. A feature is a measurable property used by the model. In face recognition, features might include the distance between eyes, face shape, nose length or skin tone. In a business dataset, features might include customer age, purchase frequency, average order value or complaint history. The model uses the

der value or complaint history. The model uses these features to learn relationships and make predictions.
Deep learning is a subfield of machine learning. It uses neural network architectures that can learn patterns from raw or less manually processed data. The major advantage is that the model can automatically learn useful features. For example, instead of manually defining eye shape and nose length for face recognition, a deep learning model can learn visual patterns directly from many image

can learn visual patterns directly from many images. This is one reason deep learning has become important in computer vision, speech recognition, natural language processing and generative AI.
Generative AI and large language models are built using deep learning. Tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini and similar systems are trained on very large datasets and use neural networks with many parameters. They can generate text, summarize documents, answer questions, write code and help with creative tasks.

estions, write code and help with creative tasks. However, they still require careful use because they can make mistakes, produce unsupported claims or reflect bias from training data. A responsible user should verify important information and understand the limits of the system.
Figure 1. Relationship between AI, machine learning, deep learning and generative AI.
5. Past, Present and Future of AI
5.1 Four Waves of AI Development
The lecture presented AI history as a series of waves. The first w

ented AI history as a series of waves. The first wave began in the 1950s and 1960s. The term artificial intelligence is commonly associated with John McCarthy and the 1956 Dartmouth conference. During this period, researchers developed early concepts such as symbolic reasoning, expert systems, Boolean logic and ideas connected with the Turing machine. At the time, computers were limited compared with today, so much of the work was conceptual rather than large-scale applied AI.
The second wave oc

er than large-scale applied AI.
The second wave occurred around the 1980s and included more formal machine learning methods. Algorithms such as multilayer perceptrons, support vector machines, random forests, fuzzy logic and genetic algorithms became important. These methods allowed systems to learn from data, but practical use was still limited by data availability, storage and computation. Many organizations did not yet have the digital data infrastructure needed for large AI projects.
The thi

frastructure needed for large AI projects.
The third wave emerged around the 2000s with the growth of the internet, cloud computing, big data and Internet of Things devices. More data became digitally available. Search engines, e-commerce platforms and social media systems began using machine learning to rank information, recommend products, personalize content and analyze user behavior. AI became more applied because organizations could collect and process larger datasets.
The fourth wave is th

and process larger datasets.
The fourth wave is the current wave, driven by deep learning, generative AI, large language models, high-performance GPUs and large-scale cloud infrastructure. This wave has made AI visible to the public because people can interact directly with tools that write text, generate images, summarize information and assist with coding. It has also made AI a strategic priority for governments, universities, businesses and public sector organizations.
Table 5. Historical wav

blic sector organizations.
Table 5. Historical waves of AI development.
5.2 Future Direction of AI
The lecture described the future of AI as highly transformative. One expected direction is the greater use of robots. Robots may become more common in factories, warehouses, healthcare, service industries and homes. Humanoid robots are being developed to perform tasks in environments designed for humans. However, widespread robot adoption will depend not only on technical ability but also on cost,

d not only on technical ability but also on cost, safety, regulation and public acceptance.
Autonomous transportation is another important future direction. Driver assistance and partial autonomy already exist, and further improvements may increase the number of autonomous vehicles. This could reduce accidents and improve efficiency, but it could also affect jobs such as taxi drivers, delivery drivers and transport operators. The lecture highlighted that AI may remove some jobs while creating ne

ted that AI may remove some jobs while creating new ones. This means society needs reskilling, training and policy planning rather than only technological optimism.
Healthcare is also likely to change. AI-assisted surgery, robotic precision and digital imaging can improve accuracy. A robot does not experience hand tremor in the same way as a human, and digital microscopes can provide detailed visual information beyond normal human vision. AI can also support disease detection, personalized medic

also support disease detection, personalized medicine and decision making. However, healthcare AI must be used carefully because mistakes can have serious consequences. Human oversight, validation and ethical review remain essential.
Cybersecurity will become increasingly important as AI becomes more powerful. AI can help defend systems by identifying unusual patterns, detecting attacks and testing vulnerabilities. At the same time, attackers can also use AI to automate malicious activity. This

also use AI to automate malicious activity. This creates a cycle in which security teams must use advanced tools to stay ahead. AI infrastructure therefore needs strong monitoring, access control, governance, testing and incident response.
6. Data, Computation and Infrastructure
6.1 Data Growth and IoT
A major theme in the lecture was the growth of data. Modern society produces huge amounts of digital information through phones, laptops, social media, search engines, e-commerce websites, smartw

media, search engines, e-commerce websites, smartwatches, sensors, cameras and connected devices. The Internet of Things refers to devices that communicate with each other and collect information without constant human input. Smart thermostats, cleaning robots, fitness watches, industrial sensors and connected vehicles are all examples.
The value of data depends on how it is used. A company may have millions of customer records, but the records are not useful unless the company can extract insig

re not useful unless the company can extract insight. AI systems help identify patterns, trends and predictions from large datasets. For example, a retailer can use data to predict demand and avoid stock shortages. A city can use sensor data to monitor traffic, air quality or energy consumption. A wildfire monitoring system can combine temperature, humidity, rain and wind data to estimate risk.
Data volume also creates responsibility. Organizations must consider privacy, security, consent, fairn

ns must consider privacy, security, consent, fairness and data quality. Bad data can lead to bad decisions. Biased data can produce unfair systems. Incomplete data can reduce accuracy. Therefore, data preparation, cleaning, governance and documentation are core parts of AI development. This is why Week 2 and Week 3 of the course focus on data analysis, feature engineering, preprocessing and algorithms.
6.2 GPU-Based Computation
The lecture explained that modern AI has grown quickly partly becaus

ned that modern AI has grown quickly partly because of high computational power. Traditional central processing units, or CPUs, are general-purpose processors. A laptop may have a small number of CPU cores. Graphics processing units, or GPUs, are designed for parallel processing and can perform many operations at the same time. This makes them useful for training deep learning models, where millions or billions of mathematical operations must be repeated across large datasets.
Large language mod

repeated across large datasets.
Large language models require especially large amounts of computation. Training a model can involve thousands of GPUs, large data centers and significant electricity. This means AI infrastructure is not only a software issue. It is also a hardware, energy, cost and sustainability issue. Organizations need to decide whether to use public cloud services, private servers, hybrid infrastructure or third-party AI APIs. The best choice depends on data sensitivity, budge

The best choice depends on data sensitivity, budget, scale, latency, compliance and technical expertise.
It is important to understand that not every AI project needs massive infrastructure. A small business can build a basic machine learning model on a normal computer or use a cloud service. A university project may run on a single GPU. A large enterprise building a private large language model for thousands of employees may need more serious infrastructure. The challenge for AI leaders is to m

frastructure. The challenge for AI leaders is to match the technical solution to the business problem rather than overbuilding or underestimating requirements.
Table 6. Common infrastructure options for AI projects.
7. Applications of AI
7.1 Smart Cities and Smart Homes
Smart cities use data and AI to improve quality of life, public services and resource management. Sensors can monitor traffic flow, energy consumption, air quality, water usage, parking availability and public safety. AI can then

arking availability and public safety. AI can then help analyze the data and support decisions. For example, traffic lights can be adjusted based on congestion patterns, maintenance can be scheduled before infrastructure fails, and emergency services can use predictive information to respond faster.
Smart homes are a smaller-scale version of the same concept. A smart home may include automated lighting, smart locks, security cameras, thermostats, cleaning robots and connected appliances. AI can

cleaning robots and connected appliances. AI can help these devices respond to user behavior. A cleaning robot may map a room and plan a path. A security camera may recognize a person or detect unusual movement. A thermostat may learn temperature preferences and adjust settings automatically. In the future, cooking appliances or domestic robots may prepare meals based on user selection, dietary needs and available ingredients.
The benefit of smart environments is convenience and efficiency, but

rt environments is convenience and efficiency, but the risks include privacy, surveillance, cybersecurity and overdependence. A smart lock or camera must be secure because it controls access to a private space. Data collected from homes and cities can reveal sensitive patterns about people. Therefore, smart city and smart home AI must be designed with privacy and security from the beginning.
7.2 Healthcare, Education and Business
Healthcare applications of AI include medical image analysis, dise

cations of AI include medical image analysis, disease prediction, surgery support, patient monitoring, drug discovery and personalized medicine. The instructor used brain tumor detection from MRI images as an example. A deep learning model can classify whether a tumor is present, identify the tumor type and locate the affected region. These systems can support doctors, but they should not be treated as complete replacements for professional medical judgement.
AI in education includes personalize

al judgement.
AI in education includes personalized learning, virtual tutors, assessment support, language translation and assistive learning tools. A single classroom includes students with different backgrounds, prior knowledge and learning speeds. A human instructor must balance the lecture for the whole group. A personalized AI learning system can adapt explanations, examples and practice questions to an individual learner. A virtual tutor can answer questions in real time, although accuracy

n answer questions in real time, although accuracy and academic integrity must be considered. AI can also support learners with visual or hearing impairments through transcription, translation and accessible content generation.
Business applications include targeted advertisement, demand forecasting, recruitment support, fraud detection, personalized shopping and customer service automation. Targeted advertising uses behavior and search data to present relevant advertisements. Demand forecasting

resent relevant advertisements. Demand forecasting helps businesses decide what to stock before items run out. Recruitment systems can filter large numbers of applications, although organizations must ensure fairness and avoid biased screening. Fraud detection uses pattern recognition to identify unusual transactions. Personalized shopping systems recommend products based on customer behavior and preferences.
Table 7. Examples of AI applications and associated risks.
8. Practical Exercise
8.1 Ex

and associated risks.
8. Practical Exercise
8.1 Exercise Aim
This practical exercise is designed for students who want a simple hands-on activity connected to Lecture 1. It does not require advanced machine learning knowledge. The aim is to use a small dataset of potential business AI use cases and classify them by domain, AI type, expected benefit and risk level. The activity helps students understand that AI project selection is not only about technical possibility. It also involves business v

technical possibility. It also involves business value, data availability, risk and infrastructure requirements.
The dataset can be copied into a CSV file and uploaded to a GitHub repository along with a README file. Students can then write a simple Python script or notebook to load the data, summarize it and create basic charts. This exercise supports project planning because it encourages students to compare possible AI applications before selecting a topic.
8.2 Associated Dataset
Table 8. Sam

cting a topic.
8.2 Associated Dataset
Table 8. Sample dataset for the practical exercise.
8.3 Python Practice Steps
Students can use the following steps in a Python file or Jupyter notebook. The purpose is to practice simple data handling, not to build a complex model. The exercise can be extended later by adding more rows, more columns or a basic classifier that predicts infrastructure need from the other columns.
Create a folder named ai-lecture-1-practical-exercise.
Place ai_use_case_dataset.

e-1-practical-exercise.
Place ai_use_case_dataset.csv inside the folder.
Create a Python file named analyze_use_cases.py.
Install pandas and matplotlib if they are not already available.
Load the CSV file and inspect the data.
Group the data by domain and calculate average benefit and risk scores.
Write a short interpretation of which use cases appear most valuable and which appear most risky.
Example Python code:
import pandas as pd

# Load the dataset
df = pd.read_csv('ai_use_case_dataset.csv'

dataset
df = pd.read_csv('ai_use_case_dataset.csv')

# Display basic information
print(df.head())
print(df.groupby('Domain')[['BenefitScore', 'RiskScore']].mean())

# Identify high-benefit and high-risk use cases
high_priority = df[(df['BenefitScore'] >= 4) & (df['RiskScore'] >= 4)]
print('High benefit and high risk use cases:')
print(high_priority[['UseCaseID', 'Domain', 'UseCase', 'InfrastructureNeed']])

8.4 GitHub README Content
The following README text can be placed in a GitHub repository

README text can be placed in a GitHub repository with the dataset. This makes the exercise understandable to other students or reviewers.
# AI Use Case Dataset - Lecture 1 Practical Exercise

This repository contains a small educational dataset for practicing the selection and comparison of artificial intelligence use cases. The dataset is based on Lecture 1 topics from Managing AI Development and Infrastructure.

## Files
- `ai_use_case_dataset.csv`: sample AI use cases across healthcare, educ

.csv`: sample AI use cases across healthcare, education, retail, cybersecurity, smart homes, banking, transportation and HR.
- `analyze_use_cases.py` or `notebook.ipynb`: optional analysis script or notebook created by the student.

## Suggested Practice Steps
1. Load the CSV file using pandas.
2. Print the first five rows of the dataset.
3. Count how many use cases appear in each domain.
4. Calculate the average BenefitScore and RiskScore.
5. Create a simple bar chart of average risk by domain.

eate a simple bar chart of average risk by domain.
6. Select one use case and write a short paragraph explaining the data, infrastructure and ethical issues.

## Example Command
```bash
python analyze_use_cases.py
```

## Learning Outcome
By completing this exercise, students practice connecting AI concepts to practical project selection, data requirements, infrastructure needs and risk management.

9. Key Takeaways and Self-Assessment
9.1 Key Takeaways
AI is a broad field focused on enabling ma

keaways
AI is a broad field focused on enabling machines to perform tasks associated with intelligence, while machine learning is a subfield where systems learn patterns from data.
Deep learning has accelerated modern AI because it can learn complex features from large datasets, especially when supported by GPUs and cloud infrastructure.
AI applications are powerful but usually task-focused, so leaders must understand data, context, risk and infrastructure before deploying them.
AI is being appl

astructure before deploying them.
AI is being applied across smart cities, healthcare, education, business, cybersecurity, transportation and manufacturing, but each use case has different ethical and operational risks.
Successful AI adoption requires continuous learning, monitoring, improvement and responsible governance rather than one-time installation of a model.
9.2 Multiple Choice Questions for Self-Assessment
Question 1: Which statement best describes the relationship between AI, machine

st describes the relationship between AI, machine learning and deep learning?
A. Deep learning is broader than AI.
B. AI is a broad field, machine learning is a subfield of AI, and deep learning is a subfield of machine learning.
C. Machine learning and AI are completely unrelated.
D. Deep learning only means writing rule-based code.
Correct answer: B.
Question 2: Why are GPUs important for modern deep learning?
A. They remove the need for data.
B. They are mainly used to write essays.
C. They s

B. They are mainly used to write essays.
C. They support parallel processing, which helps train large neural networks faster.
D. They prevent all AI errors.
Correct answer: C.
Question 3: What is a key risk when using AI for recruitment or CV screening?
A. The system may reflect bias in historical hiring data.
B. The system cannot read text.
C. AI always makes completely random decisions.
D. Recruitment systems do not use data.
Correct answer: A.

Appendix A. Quality Checklist Used for the Report
The report begins with an introduction and follows a standard reading-material structure.
Headings and sub-headings are used to divide the text into clear sections.
Tables and a diagram-style figure are included with captions to improve readability.
A practical exercise, dataset and GitHub-ready README are included.
Key takeaways, MCQs and references are provided for re

takeaways, MCQs and references are provided for revision.
The document was formatted using Times New Roman, 10-point body text and single spacing.
